data.title: Rebuilding fire-ravaged Wang Fuk Court ‘not practical’, Hong Kong officials say
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities estimate that acquiring all homes in the fire-ravaged Wang Fuk Court will cost around HK$6 billion (US$770 million), with officials saying that rebuilding on the same site was “not really very practical”.
Deputy Financial Secretary Michael Wong Wai-lun said on Saturday that the site was expected to be repurposed for community facilities rather than housing, citing overseas examples where sites of similar tragedies were rarely redeveloped for residential use. He stressed that “no one will earn big money” from the land in future.
“In this moment, we feel on-site reconstruction is not really very practical,” Wong said on a radio programme. “You look at some experiences abroad, they wouldn’t do that.”
His remarks followed the release of a government survey on Friday concerning the deadly blaze on November 26, which claimed 161 lives and displaced nearly 5,000 people. The study outlined nine rehousing options for residents, including a “flat-for-flat” exchange or a cash buyout.
Wong explained that rebuilding on the original Tai Po site would take at least nine to 10 years due to the time required for title consolidation and demolition, whereas alternative sites such as Chung Nga Road West could be ready for occupation by 2029.
He urged social workers to help residents understand the practical constraints of on-site redevelopment, noting that while emotional attachments were understandable, the logistics made reconstruction unfeasible compared with other options.
“We hope what social workers do is explain the facts to owners,” Wong said, describing the arrangements as a “special of the special” scenario given the use of public funds.
He said the site could instead help meet demand for community facilities in Tai Po and across the New Territories East.
On the buyout option, the survey cited estimates from the Hong Kong Institute of Surveyors of HK$6,000 per square foot for flats with unpaid land premiums and HK$8,000 for those with premiums settled.

Wong clarified that these figures referred to saleable area rather than gross floor area.
“I must emphasise that the real acquisition price has not been set yet, but we must have a reference price to propose; otherwise saying that is meaningless,” he said.
He noted that if the government proceeded with the buyout based on those reference prices, the total cost would be “close to HK$6 billion”. As the donation fund still held over HK$2 billion, most of the acquisition expenses would have to come from public coffers.
Wong added that the “flat-for-flat” arrangement would allow residents to exchange their damaged homes for new units of equivalent value without any cash transaction, providing more flexibility for those less comfortable handling large sums.
The nine rehousing options ranged from the earliest move-in opportunity at Shing Chi Court in Kowloon Bay by September 2026 to new flats at Chung Nga Road West in Tai Po by 2029, with other choices available in Tung Chung, Kai Tak and Tseung Kwan O.
Addressing concerns about a potential “gap period” between the expiry of the two-year rent subsidy and completion of the new flats, Deputy Chief Secretary for Administration Warner Cheuk Wing-hing said the government must handle the donation fund prudently but did not rule out further help.
“If I want to continue to provide a rent subsidy there … it is not impossible,” Cheuk said.
Cheuk added that authorities would need time to assess the safety of the damaged structures before allowing residents to re-enter the site.
“We have to do a safety assessment, which will take two to three months no matter what,” he said.

data.title: Iran protests: a day-by-day guide to the unfolding crisis
data.description:
Demonstrations broke out in Iran on December 28 and have spread nationwide as protesters vent their increasing discontent over the Islamic Republic’s faltering economy and the collapse of its currency.
Dozens of people have been killed and thousands arrested as the daily protests have grown and the government seeks to contain them.
While the initial focus had been on issues like spikes in the prices of food staples and the country’s staggering annual inflation rate, protesters have now begun chanting anti-government statements as well.


Here is how the protests developed:
December 28: Protests break out in two major markets in downtown Tehran, after the Iranian rial plunged to 1.42 million to the US dollar, a record low, compounding inflationary pressure and pushing up the prices of food and other daily necessities.

The government had raised prices for nationally subsidised petrol in early December, increasing discontent.
December 29: Central Bank head Mohammad Reza Farzin resigns as the protests in Tehran spread to other cities. Police fire tear gas to disperse protesters in the capital.

December 30: As protests spread to include more cities as well as several university campuses, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian meets with a group of business leaders to listen to their demands and pledges his administration will “not spare any effort for solving problems” with the economy.
December 31: Iran appoints Abdolnasser Hemmati as the country’s new central bank governor. Officials in southern Iran say that protests in the city of Fasa turned violent after crowds broke into the governor’s office and injured police officers.

January 1: The protests’ first fatalities are officially reported, with authorities saying at least seven people have been killed. The most intense violence appears to be in Azna, a city in Iran’s Lorestan province, where videos posted online purport to show objects in the street ablaze and gunfire echoing as people shouted: “Shameless! Shameless!”
The semi-official Fars news agency reports three people were killed. Other protesters are reported killed in Bakhtiari and Isfahan provinces while a 21-year-old volunteer in the paramilitary Revolutionary Guard’s Basij force was killed in Lorestan.

January 2: US President Donald Trump raises the stakes, writing on his Truth Social platform that if Iran “violently kills peaceful protesters”, the United States “will come to their rescue”. The warning, only months after American forces bombed Iranian nuclear sites, includes the assertion, without elaboration, that: “We are locked and loaded and ready to go.”
Protests, meantime, expand to reach more than 100 locations in 22 of Iran’s 31 provinces, according to the US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency.

January 3: Iran’s Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei says “rioters must be put in their place”, in what is seen as a green light for security forces to begin more aggressively putting down the demonstrations. Protests expand to more than 170 locations in 25 provinces, with at least 15 people killed and 580 arrested, HRANA reports.
January 6: Protesters conduct a sit-in at Tehran’s Grand Bazaar until security forces disperse them using tear gas. The death toll rises to 36, including two members of Iranian security forces, according to HRANA. Demonstrations have reached over 280 locations in 27 of Iran’s 31 provinces.

January 8: Following a call from Iran’s exiled crown prince, a mass of people shout from their windows and take to the streets in an overnight protest. The government responds by blocking the internet and international telephone calls, in a bid to cut off the country of 85 million from outside influence.
January 9: Iran signals a crackdown is coming, but protesters again demonstrate. HRANA says violence around the demonstrations has killed at least 65 people while more than 2,300 others have been detained.

data.title: Protest fury pushes Iran to the edge of ‘social revolution’
data.description:
Expectations of change at the top of Iran’s Islamic Republic are growing as nationwide protests over surging living costs unite an unusually broad swathe of society – from pro-regime merchants and the conservative rural poor to the cosmopolitan urban middle class – for the first time since the 1979 revolution.
Fuelled by the rial’s dramatic collapse following Iran’s 12-day conflict with Israel and the United States in June, the unrest has become a lightning rod for public anger at corruption, economic mismanagement and foreign policy failures under the 86-year-old Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
In the absence of any coordinated opposition, analysts say the demonstrations are being propelled by a deep sense of social injustice at the growing gulf between ordinary Iranians struggling to survive and the insulated privileges of the ruling elite.

Farzan Sabet, a managing researcher at the Geneva-based Global Governance Centre, warned that “conditions in Iran today are extreme and will likely continue deteriorating” this year.
“Foreseeable events could create the conditions for further foreign intervention, military and elite action and defection, and escalating protests,” Sabet said in a social media post on December 29, a day after the protests erupted.
He believes the prospects for systemic change are now “at their highest since the Islamic Republic’s first decade of revolution and war – and growing”.
Esfandyar Batmanghelidj, founder of the London-based Bourse and Bazaar Foundation, similarly argued that “progress is being made towards some kind of fundamental political change in Iran”.
Writing on social media, he stressed on Wednesday that Iranians were not seeking a political revolution but a “social revolution”, demanding “economic opportunity and social dignity” alongside political rights.


The unrest marks the third major wave of anti-regime protests since 2018. Observers expect authorities to again resort to arrests and force, which Khamenei and other hardliners have framed as responses to “enemy-sponsored riots”.
But a violent crackdown by the Revolutionary Guards or their Basij paramilitary wings risks triggering the military intervention that US President Donald Trump has threatened in the event of civilian bloodshed.
Meanwhile, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian’s government remains hamstrung by the UN Security Council’s reimposition in September of sanctions – and by Khamenei’s overarching control of key policy decisions.
Confronted with a ballooning budget deficit and falling oil prices, Pezeshkian’s budget proposal in late December sought to implement long-delayed structural reforms, including unifying the rial’s overvalued official exchange rate with the open market and cutting roughly US$10 billion in opaque subsidies favouring select importers and producers.
Subsidies on bread were also trimmed back and imported petroleum products pegged to market prices in a bid to stabilise the economy. But the new measures have largely just shifted the financial pain to households.

Economists expect inflation to surge well above the 52 per cent officially reported in December. The administration has tried to ease the blow with a universal monthly stipend of US$7, a fraction of the US$105 monthly minimum wage.
Veteran Iran watcher Barbara Slavin of the Washington-based Stimson Centre said inflation had become “unbearable”, adding that “something has to give”.
“Ideally”, she said, Khamenei would delegate real authority to Pezeshkian to enact sweeping reforms, from rationing essentials to recalibrating foreign policy.
But internal instability has also been deepening. The death of Khamenei’s presumed successor, former president Ebrahim Raisi, in a helicopter crash in May 2024 has intensified rivalries within the regime.
Increasingly, Khamenei has come under fire for the regime’s many failures from would-be successors, including reformist ex-president Hassan Rowhani and populist former leader Mahmoud Ahmedinejad.
Critics point to the economic crisis, political repression and Iran’s apparent inability to counter Israeli and US attacks during the June conflict, which decimated the senior military leadership and rendered the country’s nuclear enrichment programme inoperable.

Analysts say the pressure from both the street and the elite has pushed the regime to a breaking point.
“The longer the event continues and becomes more extreme, the greater the chances of dramatic changes within the regime,” Dennis Citrinowicz, a former head of Iran research for Israel’s Defence Intelligence, said in a social media post on Friday.
Some Iranian commentators have floated the idea of a “Napoleon-style” strongman – an Islamist-approved autocrat who could rescue the system from collapse.
A variation on this theme, described by analyst Ali Alfoneh as the “Venezuela model”, envisions leadership change without regime collapse
Such an arrangement could arise if Iran’s collective leadership – comprising the heads of the executive, judiciary, legislature and security forces – “chooses to sacrifice Khamenei and reach an agreement with the US”, Alfoneh told This Week In Asia.

The Revolutionary Guards “appear more inclined” to maintain a civilian presidency within a collective leadership structure rather than seize direct control, according to Alfoneh, author of Political Succession in the Islamic Republic of Iran and a senior fellow of the Washington-based Arab Gulf States Institute think tank.
This could pave the way for sanctions relief, renewed Western investment, economic stabilisation and, ultimately, regime survival.
But Slavin argued that Iran needed a democratic transition through a constitutional referendum and the end of Islamist rule.
The country “has deep civil society roots and many excellent candidates – unfortunately a lot of them in [Tehran’s] Evin prison – who could compete”, she said.
In her view, Iran’s potential revival most closely mirrors “China in the 1970s and 1980s” – a period of pragmatic reform.
“I’m just not sure it can be replicated in Iran,” Slavin added.

data.title: Chinese smart eyewear makers shine at CES with focus on challenging Meta
data.description:
Dozens of Chinese makers of smart glasses dazzled the exhibition floor at the recent CES trade show in Las Vegas, where they showed lightweight, artificial intelligence-powered eyewear aimed at a category that has been largely defined by Meta Platforms’ Ray-Ban Display.
At the four-day annual trade show, which concluded on Friday, Chinese brands made up most of the roughly 60 smart eyewear exhibitors. Their products ranged from audio-first frames designed for all-day wear to augmented reality (AR) glasses that overlay digital information onto the real world.
Exhibitors included Rokid, which debuted its audio-centric, no-display product called the Rokid AI Glasses Style supported by a mix of US and Chinese large language models; Xreal, which unveiled new AR wearables such as the Xreal 1S and the video gaming-focused ROG Xreal R1; and RayNeo, which introduced its X3 Pro Project, featuring eSIM support that allowed the glasses to run without a smartphone link.
Also at CES were LLVision Technology, which showcased its Leion Hey2 translation-focused glasses; Even Realities, which featured its newly launched 36-gram Even G2 smart eyewear with a companion smart ring; and Alibaba Group Holding, which showed its Quark AI Glasses. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
Other exhibitors included Meizu, Sharge, INMO, projector maker Xgimi and earphone brand Shokz.

Chinese firms’ strong showing at CES underscored how large language models (LLMs) and multimodal AI had made it possible to embed intelligence into a fast-developing range of consumer hardware and wearables. Some already considered smart glasses as potentially the next major personal computing platforms after smartphones.
“The rise of smart glasses is riding directly on advances in AI,” said Wu Fei, founder and CEO of LLVision.
He said the Beijing-based company “did less” with its latest smart eyewear by focusing entirely on translation for now, instead of packing these products with many features. Powered by LLMs, the LLVision glasses support mixed-language input and multi-speaker conversations, with a real-time transcript floating in the user’s field of view.
According to research firm IDC, smart eyewear shipments in China reached 623,000 units in the third quarter, up 62.3 per cent from a year earlier. Audio and video-recording glasses accounted for 454,000 units, while AR and virtual reality devices made up the remaining 169,000.
Global shipments of smart glasses more than doubled in the first half of 2025, rising 110 per cent year on year, according to Counterpoint Research. Meta dominated the market with a 73 per cent share on the back of its Ray-Ban Display models. Founder Mark Zuckerberg reportedly set a 2025 sales target of 5 million units.

Chinese manufacturers, however, have quickly narrowed the gap, helped by a dense supplier ecosystem that had made it possible to squeeze increasingly complex components into a compact pair of glasses.
“So many players are entering the market,” said Chen Menghao, general manager of the innovation centre at Appotronics, a laser technology provider that develops optical engines used in display-equipped smart glasses.
At CES, Appotronics exhibited its ultra-miniaturised Dragonfly G1 Mini, a 0.2 cubic centimetre single-green engine, alongside the 0.4cc full-colour Dragonfly C1. Both components enabled a single-engine binocular design – a key step towards lighter AR glasses.
Demand from smart eyewear makers had surged, according to Chen. He said Appotronics expected 2027 to be “a very big year” for the product category, as smart glasses become “good enough” and affordable at under US$300 to enter the mainstream consumer market.
Based on customer orders and conversations across the supply chain, this appears as the early acceleration phase of AI hardware, “not the peak of a bubble”, according to a Lens Technology staff member who declined to be identified. The company supplies glass covers and structural components for consumer electronics.

Lens Technology, headquartered in central Hunan province and a supplier to Apple, was also the manufacturing partner of Hangzhou-based Rokid. Other major Chinese supply chain suppliers at CES included Goertek, O-Film and Sunny Optical.
“The evolution of chips and optical displays will be critical,” said Jason Low, a research director at Omdia. “They directly shape what smart glasses could become next.”
Chinese smart glasses makers and their suppliers are pushing each other to innovate, according to LLVision’s Wu. As each generation of components becomes smaller, lighter and more power-efficient, production costs could get lower, he added.
“Advances across smartphones, cars, communications and semiconductors have lifted China’s overall manufacturing and design capabilities,” Wu said.
That also reflected how competition among smart eyewear brands would intensify, especially when these products are adopted by a broader audience.
“The pace of growth over the past year exceeded our expectations,” said Zoro Shao, global general manager at Rokid, who spoke on the sidelines of CES.
The company expected to ship 1 million units across its product line in 2026, founder and CEO Misa Zhu Mingming told the Post in a November interview.

Chinese smart eyewear makers have also sharpened their focus on overseas markets.
About a quarter of Rokid’s orders currently come from outside China, Shao said. The US accounted for roughly half of that demand, while Asia-Pacific markets made up 30 per cent. Even Realities exclusively sells its products overseas, while Xreal said international markets accounted for about 70 per cent of its sales.
In China, the government introduced a new national subsidy covering 15 per cent of the purchase price of smart glasses priced under 6,000 yuan (US$859) for 2026, part of a broader programme aimed at stimulating consumer spending, which could further accelerate the adoption of smart glasses.

data.title: Our cheat sheet for what to do in Hong Kong, January 11-17
data.description:
Eat this
Cristal Room by Anne-Sophie Pic x Qu Lang Yuan

Anne-Sophie Pic’s Hong Kong outpost welcomes Beijing talent Li Zhanxu of Qu Lang Yuan for a two-night residency on Monday and Tuesday. Li, whose home restaurant is housed in a serene courtyard in the capital’s historic Dongsi Hutong, brings a poetic Franco-Chinese dialogue to Pic’s harbourfront establishment. Expect a seamless meld of techniques: Pic’s signature comté berlingots are receiving an oolong infusion while Alsatian venison is paired with black garlic. It is a sophisticated, texturally complex study in cross-border gastronomy.
44/F, Gloucester Tower, Landmark, 15 Queen’s Road Central, Central. Book through cristalroombyasp.com and sevenrooms.com
Drink this
Sugar

Taikoo Shing’s reliable rooftop haunt has polished its act for its 15th anniversary. While the terrace still commands those sweeping harbour views, the menu has pivoted to a playful blend of film and fusion. Mixologist Alvin Ching’s new list nods to Asian cinema classics – try the Vitasoy-spiked Initial D – while chef Wing Yuen elevates local dim sum with Japanese precision; the negi toro siu mai is a stand-out. It’s a refreshed, unpretentious spot for high-altitude sundowners.
32/F, East Hong Kong, 29 Taikoo Shing Road, Taikoo Shing
Do this
Disney on Ice

The skating spectacular returns to Hong Kong from January 23 to 25, mixing hits both old and new. While Mickey and Minnie steer the ship through classics such as Aladdin and Toy Story, the draw for younger fans is the inclusion of Encanto and Frozen II. Keep an eye out for on-ice debuts from Raya and the Last Dragon and Wish. This is polished, high-octane family entertainment that knows exactly what it’s doing.
Kai Tak Arena, Kai Tak Sports Park, Kai Tak. Tickets start at HK$128; disneyonice.com
See this
Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon

The city’s premier road race will take over the streets on January 18. A logistical leviathan, the Standard Chartered Marathon shuts major arteries from Nathan Road to the Western Harbour Crossing, funnelling thousands of runners towards a Victoria Park finish. Whether you are chasing a sub-three-hour time or just the spectacle, it remains the definitive, sweaty pulse-check of Hong Kong’s sporting calendar.
Various districts; for details, go to hkmarathon.com

data.title: ‘We do not want to be Americans’ Greenland parliament tells US
data.description:
Greenland’s political parties on Friday expressed unity in response to US President Donald Trump’s claims to the Danish territory.
“We do not want to be Americans, we do not want to be Danes, we want to be Greenlanders,” said a joint statement signed by the five parties in the Greenlandic parliament, Inatsisartut.
The parties called on the United States to end its “disdain for our country” and emphasised that “the future of Greenland must be decided by the Greenlandic people.”
Trump had reaffirmed his claim to Greenland on Friday, saying the US must act to prevent China or Russia from gaining control of the strategically important Arctic island.


Speaking to reporters during an event at the White House, he said Washington would do “something” about Greenland, whether others liked it or not. “Because if we don’t do it, Russia or China will take over Greenland, and we’re not going to have Russia or China as a neighbour,” he said.
“I would like to make a deal, you know, the easy way, but if we don’t do it the easy way, we’re going to do it the hard way,” he added.
Trump has repeatedly said he wants to bring the island, which belongs to Nato ally Denmark, under US control, citing its strategic importance and what he described as a growing presence of Russian and Chinese ships in the region.
His remarks have drawn alarm because he has not ruled out military or economic coercion. Greenland is mostly covered in ice and has a population of about 57,000.
The Danish government has firmly rejected Trump’s claim. Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen warned that a US attack on Greenland would spell the end of Nato and the post-World War II security order.

data.title: How Netflix Japanese drama Gimbap and Onigiri bridges cultures with food and romance
data.description:
Food is the starting point for romance in Gimbap and Onigiri, a new Japanese drama pairing Japanese actor Eiji Akaso and Korea’s idol-turned-actress Kang Hye-won.
Set to premiere on January 12 on Japanese television station TV Tokyo, the series follows two young adults as they navigate uncertainty in their lives and experience romance despite their different cultures.
Rather than centring on cultural clashes, the story unfolds through quiet encounters, creating a story of intimacy that is gradual and relatable.
Akaso stars as Hase Taiga, a former college track athlete who loses direction after a major setback. With his confidence shaken, Taiga drifts through life without a clear vision for the future, working part-time at a small restaurant in Japan.
“Taiga meets Park Rin through his part-time job, and the show centres on how he grows after that,” Akaso said at a press conference in Seoul.
“When I first saw the title, I was curious about what kind of story it would be. The characters are as warm and approachable as the title suggests. It is a story about people who live sincerely and love sincerely.”

Kang plays Park Rin, a Korean graduate student adjusting to life abroad. Living alone in a foreign environment, she reaches an emotional turning point after she eats a meal prepared by Taiga, a small encounter that brings her great comfort.
“I tend to appreciate happiness that comes from small things,” Kang said. “That was the feeling I had when I first read the script. It is a story where those modest joys and moments of warmth really come through.”
The drama places emphasis not on cultural differences as a form of conflict, but on the process of learning another person’s way of life. Its title, Gimbap and Onigiri, reflects that approach, featuring distinct foods rooted in cultures that are different yet similar enough to feel familiar.
Despite filming across languages and cultures, both actors said their collaboration unfolded smoothly.
“It was the best,” Akaso said, describing his behind-the-scenes chemistry with Kang. “Personally, I did not find it difficult. If anything, Kang had a harder time because she was filming in Japan. During public events like this, I have interpreters, but during filming, she had to work alone through a language barrier. I really respect how she handled that.”

Kang admitted that initially she had been worried, despite having studied Japanese for two months before filming.
“I was concerned because my language skills were not perfect,” she said. “Akaso made a real effort to look out for me, even though we spoke different languages. He helped me settle into the environment, and the staff also guided me carefully so I could understand. I felt supported throughout.”
She added that her previous experience promoting in Japan as a member of the K-pop girl group Iz*One proved helpful.
“Because I had already worked in Japan, the environment did not feel completely unfamiliar,” Kang said. “I also think that if I had not gone to Japan back then, I would not have been able to pick up the language to this level in such a short time.”
The drama arrives as Korean-Japanese co-productions continue to gain visibility through global streaming platforms. Recent examples include Japan’s Eye Love You, released internationally via Netflix, and Coupang Play’s What Comes After Love.

Akaso credited streaming services with helping sustain such collaborations.
“Platforms like Netflix have become an important bridge,” he said. “They allow people in different countries to watch each other’s work. Collaborating beyond borders through storytelling is meaningful, and I hope to continue participating in projects that connect with global production teams.”
Gimbap and Onigiri will air every Monday on TV Tokyo starting on January 12, with simultaneous worldwide streaming available on Netflix.
Read the full story at The Korea Times

data.title: China-led team replicates human embryo ‘invasion’ of the womb for the first time
data.description:
A China-led team has built the world’s first mini-womb on a chip that can fully replicate the “invasion” of human embryos in the uterus during early pregnancy.
Their 3D model on a microfluidic chip can be used to uncover the mysteries behind human embryo implantation and lead to personalised treatments for women struggling to get pregnant.
The team led by researchers from the Chinese Academy of Sciences Institute of Zoology said that while achieving a better understanding of human implantation was “crucial”, it was challenged by ethical constraints and limited access to human embryos.
“To overcome this, we developed a 3D in-chip implantation model using human blastoids or blastocysts co-cultured with a bioengineered human endometrial tissue, termed endometrioid,” the team said in a paper published in the peer-reviewed journal Cell on December 23.
“This system successfully recapitulates key events of human implantation and early post-implantation development,” the team, which included researchers from universities and medical centres in China and the United States, said.
Blastocysts are five to six-day-old embryos that consist of 100 to 200 rapidly dividing cells, including an outer cell mass that will implant into the uterus to develop into the placenta and an inner cell mass that will eventually form the baby.
Blastoids are blastocyst-like structures made from human pluripotent stem cells – which can develop into any cell type – that are scalable and genetically consistent, making them ideal for studies like drug screening.
“Human pregnancy begins with embryo implantation, initiated by a blastocyst attaching to the uterine lining – an essential step for pregnancy continuation and reproductive success,” the team said.


The implantation process involves three main steps: apposition, where the blastocysts make first contact with the uterine lining; adhesion, where it firmly bonds with the lining using specific molecules; and invasion, where the blastocysts penetrate and embed themselves into the endometrium.
Some bacteria similarly use surface proteins to bind to host cell receptors before they either invade or engulf the cells to colonise, subvert host functions or drive disease progression.
Globally, one in six adults are affected by infertility, defined by a failure to achieve pregnancy after 12 months of unprotected sexual intercourse, according to the World Health Organization.
Understanding the process of implantation is necessary to advance assisted reproductive technologies and to help patients with recurrent implantation failure (RIF), which is diagnosed after more than three unsuccessful attempts at in vitro fertilisation and embryo transfer, according to the researchers.
“However, ethical and practical limitations restrict direct studies in human natural embryos, traditionally relying on early pregnancy tissue samples from hysterectomies.”
Scientists have been developing models, including 3D endometrial organoids, to replicate human implantation, though existing models are missing features and 3D embryo-endometrium interactions.
The Chinese-led team created their womb chip by combining human blastocysts or blastoids with 3D bioengineered human endometrial tissues, which can be obtained from a single biopsy, onto a microfluidic chip.
“Our in-chip 3D endometrioid-based implantation model offers a streamlined platform that captures all major stages of implantation – apposition, attachment and invasion – as well as early post-implantation development.”




They first combined gel layers with endometrial cells to create the 3D model, which is “structurally similar” to a real endometrium. The team then introduced blastoids and obtained better observation of the entire implantation sequence than the previous 2D models.
The researchers also tested their model by introducing human blastocysts, which successfully implanted in the womb models.
When using models from patients with RIF, they found that they had a “significantly reduced blastoid implantation capability” compared with the chips obtained from fertile patients.
Using the models, they screened more than 1,000 US Food and Drug Administration-approved compounds to identify ones that improved implantation rates, addressing a gap in drug testing for RIF.
Although they created their models using cells from endometrial biopsies, the team said the system was also compatible with endometrial cells obtained non-invasively through menstrual blood.
The model still has its limits, such as a lack of vascular and immune cell components, which are essential during uterine remodelling, a process important to increase blood flow to the placenta and growing fetus.
“Together, this 3D platform enables mechanistic investigation of human implantation and implantation failure and offers a scalable approach to evaluate therapeutic strategies for improving embryo-endometrium interaction in a clinical setting,” the team said.

data.title: Review of Meta-Manus deal underlines China’s tightening grip on AI exports
data.description:
China’s review of Meta Platforms’ acquisition of artificial intelligence agent start-up Manus signals Beijing’s intent to more tightly police foreign involvement in sensitive technologies developed by Chinese entrepreneurs, as more founders move operations overseas to sidestep geopolitical scrutiny, analysts say.
The Ministry of Commerce said on Thursday that it would work with other Chinese regulators to assess whether the acquisition complied with the country’s export controls and technology transfer rules.
The review could become a high-profile test case for China’s equivalent of the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States (CFIUS), according to Winston Ma, an adjunct professor at New York University School of Law who focuses on AI and the digital economy.
CFIUS, which vets and blocks inbound investment on national security grounds, tightened oversight during Donald Trump’s first term as US president. Since then, China had developed its own framework to review foreign involvement in sensitive domestic technologies, according to Ma.
Facebook parent Meta acquired Manus, founded by Chinese entrepreneur Red Xiao Hong, in late December, betting on its AI agent technology that allowed models to autonomously carry out complex workflows with minimal human intervention.
Dai Menghao, Shanghai-based partner at King & Wood Mallesons specialising in export controls and sanctions, said the AI agent developed by Manus was “definitely something that Chinese regulators could subject to export controls”.

Dai said China had been aligning with the US in many operational aspects of export controls. “While the US has focused on AI models … China uses a very broad concept called ‘technology’ – not isolating models but the entire technology,” Dai added.
Manus relocated its headquarters to Singapore last year. Xiao was currently based there but remained a Chinese national, according to a source close to the company.
After the relocation, Manus sought to terminate its mainland-registered entities, but the process can be lengthy as it requires regulatory review of data and technology exports, which fall under China’s export control and data compliance regime.
The acquisition was completed swiftly, in about 10 days, Liu Yuan, a partner at Chinese venture capital firm ZhenFund, which invested in Manus, said previously.
Chinese regulators’ probe into the acquisition added uncertainty to the deal, but it was less about blocking a single transaction than sending a broader warning to the Chinese AI community to comply with regulations, Han Qin, CEO at investment firm Jarsy, wrote in a social media post.
Han added that relocation overseas would not place companies or founders beyond Beijing’s jurisdiction, which was a common oversight by many Chinese entrepreneurs. “For example, if the technical lead, founder, or core engineer is a Chinese citizen, China’s administrative and criminal jurisdiction over its own nationals does not automatically disappear simply because they are overseas,” Han said.
Manus declined to comment on the review.
Industry insiders were debating what would be the likely outcome of the review. A source familiar with Chinese regulators’ thinking said the deal set a “bad precedent” and could prompt Beijing to tighten its grip on critical AI technology exports.
If Manus’s relocation to Singapore was deemed problematic, the transaction could ultimately be called off by Chinese authorities, the source added.
Still, some experts said the review was unlikely to be a deal-breaker, though it highlighted how poorly prepared companies were for Chinese regulatory risks even as they carefully accounted for US scrutiny.
“The review is not the end,” according to Leslie Zhang Weihua, vice-president and legal chief at the United Energy Group. “Instead companies will have to actively communicate with Chinese regulators to alleviate their concerns.”
Wang Yiming, a policy expert at Beijing Xinzheng Market Cap Management Consulting, said Manus should conduct a structured self-assessment to identify potential risks related to technology export, data transfer and capital flows.
He said Beijing could cite several Chinese laws and regulations, including cybersecurity, data security and trade of dual-use items and technologies, which covered AI algorithms and high-performance computing technology, as well as overseas investment regulations.
Wang estimated that it would take half a year for Manus to clear Beijing’s scrutiny.
You Yunting, an intellectual property lawyer and senior partner at Shanghai Debund Law Firm, cautioned that apart from China’s technology export review, the deal could also face antitrust risks. “If this deal leads to significant advancements in Meta’s models, it could squeeze the market space for the Chinese domestic AI ecosystem,” You said.
Additional reporting by Iris Deng

data.title: ‘No taste’: Malaysia’s Life of Pi and Squid Game artists warn against AI rush
data.description:
Firdaus Hashim has spent nearly two decades breathing life into the impossible. The Malaysian visual effects (VFX) artist has won multiple accolades for his painstaking, frame-by-frame artistry and even contributed to an Oscar-winning film.
But today, the 39-year-old finds himself confronted by a force shaking the very foundations of his craft: artificial intelligence.
Since starting out in 2009, Firdaus has honed his skills through the laborious sketching of individual frames, developing an eye for realism and storytelling. He still speaks fondly of his experiences on Life of Pi, which won the Academy Award for Best Visual Effects in 2013, calling it a project “I will remember forever”.
He told This Week in Asia that one of the most unforgettable moments was working on stereoscopic footage of the hyperrealistic computer-generated tiger known as Richard Parker, which features in several of the film’s key scenes.

But as AI tools increasingly move into filmmaking, Firdaus and other Malaysian creatives say they are sceptical that the technology can “improve” films by accelerating the production process or somehow enhancing overall artistic quality.
The debate erupted last month after the producers of Duyung: Legenda Aurora (Mermaid: Legend of Aurora) – a prequel to the 2008 Malaysian fantasy Duyung – said they had used AI to “improve” the production. The comments drew a backlash, with viewers complaining that the results looked cheap and had compromised the film’s artistic integrity.
When it comes to AI, Firdaus’ main concern is what its increasing use could mean for creative careers like his.
“I kind of understand the producer’s point of view because their main task is to get a project done at a very low cost to maximise profit,” he said.
Beyond “speeding things up” or saving money, however, Firdaus said the technology had its limitations – namely that while many could use it, few were able to produce visuals that felt truly alive.
AI was evolving fast, he acknowledged, with new models appearing every few weeks.
“As VFX artists, we need to adapt,” Firdaus said. “But is there something machines still can’t replace, you ask? My answer is ‘taste’.”

‘Every detail matters’
Another Malaysian artist, Azhar Zakeriah – better known as Harzach – has also been weighing AI’s creative potential against its limitations.
The VFX specialist, whose portfolio includes global Netflix hits such as Squid Game and Stranger Things, said human judgment and experience remained essential to his craft.
Azhar began freelancing in 2010 before going full time a decade later, building a career across television, film and advertising. He said his projects with Netflix were among the most demanding, where “every detail matters”.
“When a new project comes in, it usually goes through three main stages: pre-production, production and post-production,” Azhar told This Week in Asia.
During pre-production, teams study the script, storyboards and visual references to plan out workflows and assets, often creating pre-visualisations to map complex VFX shots.
On set, VFX artists supervise production to ensure a seamless transition to the heavy post-production work that follows – compositing, 3D modelling, simulations and multiple rounds of review until each shot matches the filmmaker’s creative vision.
AI could support some of these steps, Azhar said, but only in certain limited ways such as generating references, testing visual ideas and assisting with object removal.
“AI is starting to appear in some local creative projects,” he said.
But he thinks that in film, the human touch should still guide the storytelling.


Art vs automation
Malaysia’s film industry is relatively modest by Hollywood standards. In 2024, 61 local feature films generated just over 165 million ringgit (US$40.6 million) (HK$317 million) in revenue, according to the National Film Development Corporation Malaysia.
Outside film, the broader creative sector employed around 763,100 people that year, accounting for 4.7 per cent of all jobs tracked by the Department of Statistics.
The government has been pushing hard for AI adoption, allocating nearly 5.9 billion ringgit to strengthen its research, development and commercialisation, even as uncertainty lingers among the country’s creative community.
Pirate Queen: Zheng Yi Sao, which premiered in cinemas on New Year’s Day, was billed as the local film industry’s first “fully AI-generated” feature. The 76-minute historical epic, co-produced by Malaysia’s Future Studio and Singapore’s FizzDragon, was marketed as a milestone in cost-saving innovation by its executive producer, Yoki Chin.
Production costs for large battle sequences that would have required hundreds of extras and elaborate props were reportedly much lower than they would have been otherwise.
Still, not everyone is convinced that such developments truly equate to progress.
Andy Darrel Gomes, a filmmaker and lecturer at Malaysia’s Sunway University, warned that overreliance on AI could diminish the very essence of filmmaking.
My biggest concern isn’t just jobs being replaced, we might end up producing artists incapable of independent thinking
Andy Darrel Gomes, filmmaker
Citing legendary American director Orson Welles, he said: “The biggest enemy of art is the absence of limitation”, arguing that the creative process should involve trial and error.
“My biggest concern isn’t just jobs being replaced,” Gomes told This Week in Asia. “It’s that we might end up producing artists incapable of independent thinking.”
For him, filmmaking should be a collaborative effort. But AI stripped this away, turning it into an “individualistic endeavour”.
“Every filmmaking process is meant to further refine the filmmaker,” he said. “To focus on an ‘improved’ film without developing the filmmaker is a solely industrial, product-centric pursuit.”
